title: Input & Output
overview: Programs are interactive — they ask for input, process it, and show output. In this lecture you'll master getting user input and formatting output like a pro.

blocks.title: Displaying Output with print()
blocks.body: You already know print() displays text. But print() has superpowers — you can print multiple items, change separators, and control line endings.
bullets: print() accepts multiple items separated by commas
By default, items are separated by a space
Use sep parameter to change the separator
Use end parameter to change what comes at the end (default is newline)

blocks.title: Custom Separator with sep
blocks.code:
print("apple", "banana", "cherry", sep=", ")
print("one", "two", "three", sep=" - ")
print("A", "B", "C", sep="")
blocks.expectedOutput:
apple, banana, cherry
one - two - three
ABC

blocks.title: Custom Ending with end
blocks.code:
print("Loading", end="")
print(".", end="")
print(".", end="")
print(".")
print("Done!")

blocks.title: Design Your Output
blocks.instructions: Use print() with sep to display numbers 1 through 5 separated by arrows: "1 → 2 → 3 → 4 → 5"
blocks.starterCode:
# Print numbers with arrow separator
print(1, 2, 3, 4, 5, sep="")
blocks.hint: Put your numbers as separate arguments to print(). Set sep=" → " with the arrow symbol.

blocks.title: Getting Input with input()
blocks.body: The `input()` function waits for the user to type something and press Enter. It **always returns a string** — even if the user types a number.
bullets: input() pauses the program and waits for the user
You can show a prompt: input("Enter your name: ")
The return value is always a string
Use int(input()) to get an integer from the user

blocks.title: input() is Like a Receptionist
blocks.text: You ask a question (the prompt), then wait for the person to answer (type and press Enter). The receptionist (input()) hands you the answer on a piece of paper (a string). If you need it as a number, you must convert it yourself.

blocks.title: Getting and Using Input
blocks.code:
name = input("Enter your name: ")
print("Hello, " + name + "!")
blocks.expectedOutput:
Enter your name: Lakshanya
Hello, Lakshanya!

blocks.title: Converting Input to Number
blocks.code:
age_str = input("Enter your age: ")
age = int(age_str)
next_year = age + 1
print(f"Next year you will be {next_year}")
blocks.expectedOutput:
Enter your age: 20
Next year you will be 21

blocks.title: Shortcut: int(input())
blocks.code:
age = int(input("Enter your age: "))
print(f"You are {age} years old")
blocks.expectedOutput:
Enter your age: 20
You are 20 years old

blocks.question: What happens if the user types 'twenty' instead of 20, and you try int(input())?
blocks.answer: Python gives a ValueError because 'twenty' cannot be converted to an integer. This is called an exception.

blocks.title: Student Details Form
blocks.instructions: Ask the user for their name (string), age (integer), and city (string). Then print all details in a formatted card using f-strings.
blocks.starterCode:
# Get student details
name = input("Enter your name: ")
age = 
city = 

# Print details card
blocks.solution:
name = input("Enter your name: ")
age = int(input("Enter your age: "))
city = input("Enter your city: ")

print(f"--- Student Card ---")
print(f"Name: {name}")
print(f"Age: {age}")
print(f"City: {city}")
blocks.hint: Use int(input()) for numbers. Use f-strings to format the output nicely.
blocks.expectedOutput:
--- Student Card ---
Name: Lakshanya
Age: 20
City: Chennai

blocks.title: Formatting with f-Strings
blocks.body: **f-strings** (formatted strings) are the cleanest way to combine text and variables. Just prefix your string with `f` and put expressions inside `{}`.
bullets: Prefix the string with f: f"Hello {name}"
Any expression works inside {}: math, function calls, variables
They are faster and more readable than + concatenation

blocks.title: f-String Examples
blocks.code:
name = "Lakshanya"
age = 20

print(f"Hello, {name}!")
print(f"{name} is {age} years old")
print(f"In 5 years, {name} will be {age + 5}")
blocks.expectedOutput:
Hello, Lakshanya!
Lakshanya is 20 years old
In 5 years, Lakshanya will be 25

blocks.title: f-Strings vs Concatenation
blocks.code:
# f-strings are cleaner:
name = "Alice"
score = 95
print(f"{name} scored {score}%")

# vs concatenation:
print(name + " scored " + str(score) + "%")

blocks.title: Simple Calculator
blocks.instructions: Ask the user for two numbers. Convert them to integers. Print the sum, difference, and product using f-strings.
blocks.starterCode:
# Get two numbers
num1 = int(input("Enter first number: "))
num2 = 

# Calculate and print
blocks.solution:
num1 = int(input("Enter first number: "))
num2 = int(input("Enter second number: "))

print(f"Sum: {num1 + num2}")
print(f"Difference: {num1 - num2}")
print(f"Product: {num1 * num2}")
blocks.hint: Use int(input()) for both numbers. Use f-strings with {} to show calculations.
blocks.expectedOutput:
Enter first number: 10
Enter second number: 5
Sum: 15
Difference: 5
Product: 50

keyTakeaways.text: print() can take multiple items, sep changes separator, end changes line ending
input() gets text from the user, always returns a string
Use int(input()) to get numbers from user input
f-strings embed variables with {} and are cleaner than concatenation
int() fails with ValueError if the input is not a valid number
Combine input, conversion, and f-strings for interactive programs

whatYouLearned: How to format print() output with sep and end
How to get user input with input()
How to convert string input to integers
How to format strings with f-strings
The difference between f-strings and concatenation
How to build a simple interactive calculator
